elements.sent-2411-001: We propose a new method to measure the evolution of the neutron skin thickness between different isotopes with small statistical and systematic uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-002: We consider antiproton--nucleus interactions close to the production threshold of $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ and $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ pairs.

elements.sent-2411-004: Within a simple geometrical picture we show that the double ratio for the production of $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ and $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ pairs for two different isotopes are directly related to the variation of the neutron skin thickness between the two nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-005: Performing high statistics calculations with the Gie\ss en Boltzmann--Uehling--Uhlenbeck (GiBUU) transport model for several isotope pairs covering a wide range of elements we verify a strong correlation between the double ratio from the full transport simulations and the schematic model.
elements.sent-2411-006: This correlation enables us to quantify the high potential of the proposed method for precise studies of neutron skin variations in isotope chains.
elements.sent-2411-007: To describe neutron rich matter which eventually appears in dense stellar objects, knowledge on the isospin part of the EoS is indispensable.
elements.sent-2411-008: Luckily, the isospin dependence of the EoS correlates strongly with the distribution of neutrons in nuclei, see \cite{PhysRevC.86.015803,BALDO2016203,doi:10.1063/PT.3.4247 and references therein.}

elements.sent-2411-032: In this work we present a novel method to explore the variation of the neutron skin between two different isotopes of a given element with high precision and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-033: For that purpose we consider antiproton--nucleus interactions close to the thresholds of $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ and $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ pair production.
elements.sent-2411-034: At low energies, $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ pairs are produced in $\overline{\text{p }$+p collisions, while $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ pairs can only be produced in $\overline{\text{p }$+n interactions.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Unlike other probes, antiprotons are strongly absorbed in the nuclear periphery and, therefore, are particularly sensitive to small variations of the nuclear skin.
elements.sent-2411-036: Measuring the probabilities p$_{\Lambda\overline{\Lambda }$ and p$_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ for the two processes for a reference isotope (I) and a second isotope (II), allows to determine the double ratio \begin{equation}DR = \frac{p^{II}_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}\Big/p^{II}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }} }{p^{I}_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}\Big/p^{I}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}}. \label{eq:dr}\end{equation} Such a double ratio does not require the absolute determination of cross sections and also the different energy dependence for the $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ and $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ channels is eliminated.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, measuring both the production probabilities for a given isotope simultaneously, many experimental uncertainties cancel or can be significantly reduced.

elements.sent-2411-045: For simplicity we consider a nearly central antiproton--nucleus collision (see left part of \autoref{fig:NeuS_scheme ).}
elements.sent-2411-046: Close to threshold, the probability $p^{I _{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ to produce a $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ pair within the reference nucleus (I) can be written as \begin{equation}p^{I}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}=\kappa_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }} \cdot \frac{\rho (p)}{\rho (p)+\rho (n)} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}}{\sigma_\text{tot}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here $\sigma_\text{tot}$ denotes the total ${\overline{\text{p +A}$ cross section and $\sigma_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ is the elementary $\overline{\text{p +{\text{p \rightarrow \Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ cross section.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The factor $\kappa_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ describes the loss of $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ pairs due to absorptive re-scattering.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Similarly, the production probability of $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ pairs can be approximated by \begin{equation}p^{I}_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}=\kappa_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }} \cdot \frac{\rho (n)}{\rho (p)+\rho (n)} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}}{\sigma_\text{tot}}. \label{eq:siglamI}\end{equation} Because of the large annihilation cross section of antibaryons in nuclei, both, $\kappa_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ and $\kappa_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ are dominated by the $\overline{\Lambda }$ absorption.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: We now turn to a isotope (II) with a larger neutron number and hence a more extended neutron distribution.
elements.sent-2411-052: For simplicity, we assume that the proton distribution remains identical to the one of nucleus (I) and that the neutron distribution is only extended by an additional halo $\Delta_\text{n}$ at the surface, see pink area in the right part of \autoref{fig:NeuS_scheme .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Such a situation is approximately found in isotope chains of heavy nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-054: In such a scenario, the production of $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ pairs is reduced by the absorption probability $p_\text{abs}$ of the incident antiprotons within this additional neutron skin $\Delta_\text{n}$: \begin{equation}p^{II}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}=(1-p_\text{abs}) \cdot \kappa_{II} \cdot \frac{\rho (p)}{\rho (p)+\rho (n)} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}}{\sigma_\text{tot}}. \label{eq:pll2}\end{equation} The absorption probability $p_\text{abs}$ can be expressed in terms of the total $\overline{\text{p +\text{n}$ reaction cross section $\sigma_{\overline{\text{p \text{n }$~\cite{Workman:2022ynf and the integrated skin density $\int_{\Delta_\text{n \rho_\text{n}\, \text{d r_\text{n}$ of the additional neutron skin of nucleus (II) with respect to the reference nucleus (I): \begin{equation}1-p_\text{abs}\approx \exp \Big\{ -\sigma_{\overline{\text{p}}\text{n}} \cdot \int_{\Delta_\text{n}} \rho_\text{n} \text{d}r_\text{n} \Big\} \label{eq:pabs}\end{equation} Like the incoming antiprotons, the produced $\overline{\Lambda }$ are also absorbed in the additional neutron layer.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: With this simplification we can express \autoref{eq:pll2 as \begin{equation}p^{II}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }} = \kappa_{n} \cdot (1-p_{abs}) \cdot p^{I}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}. \label{eqLLII}\end{equation} The production of $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ pairs gains an additional component from the additional neutron skin.}
elements.sent-2411-057: On the other hand, the contribution from the inner part of the nucleus (II) is reduced by the loss of antiprotons in the additional neutron layer: \begin{equation}p^{II}_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }} = \kappa_{II} \cdot {p_abs} \cdot {\frac{\sigma_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}}{\sigma_tot}} + (1-p_abs) \cdot \kappa_{II}\frac{\rho (n)}{\rho (p)+\rho (n)} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}}{\sigma_tot}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: Here, we assume that the loss of outgoing $\Sigma^-$ and$/$or $\overline{\Lambda }$ for pairs produced in the additional neutron skin is the same as for pairs produced within the core nucleus.
elements.sent-2411-059: We thus obtain for the double ratio \begin{equation}DR = \frac{p^{II}_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}\Big/p^{II}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}}{p^{I}_{\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }}\Big/p^{I}_{\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }}} = \frac{p_\text{abs} \cdot \frac{\rho (p)+\rho (n)}{\rho (n)} + \Big(1-p_\text{abs}\Big) }{1-p_\text{abs}}\end{equation} With the simplifying assumption $\rho (p)$ = $\frac{Z {N}\cdot\rho (n)$, where Z denotes the element number and N the average neutron number of the two isotopes, we finally find for the double ratio the expression \begin{equation}DR = \frac{1+p_\text{abs}{\cdot}Z/N}{1-p_\text{abs}} \label{eq:dr01}\end{equation} Since the variation of the neutron skin thickness is rather small, the additional absorption probability $p_\text{abs}$ is also small.}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this case, we can expand \autoref{eq:dr01 and obtain a linear relation between the double ratio DR and the absorption probability of the incident antiprotons within the increased neutron skin $\Delta_\text{n}$. \begin{equation}DR \approx 1+(1+\frac{Z}{N}) \cdot p_\text{abs}. \label{eq:dr01b}\end{equation} This expression is nearly independent of the considered nuclei and it signals that DR is a direct measure of the increment of (integrated) neutron skin thickness.}

elements.sent-2411-187: We propose a new method to study the evolution of the neutron periphery between different isotopes by considering antiproton--nucleus interactions close to the production threshold of $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ and $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ pairs.
elements.sent-2411-188: Within a simple geometrical picture we show that the double ratio for the production of $\Sigma^-\overline{\Lambda }$ and $\Lambda \overline{\Lambda }$ pairs for two different isotopes are related to the variation of the neutron skin thickness between the two nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-189: This correlation enables us to quantify the potential of the proposed method for precise studies of neutron skin variations in isotope chains.
elements.sent-2411-190: However, this definition is not unique because of possibly significant density variations both, in the nuclear periphery and in the center \cite{PhysRevC.61.044326 (see the discussion in \autoref{sec:sensitivity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Indeed, for example a recent nonlocal dispersive optical model analysis suggests a neutron skin for $^{208}$Pb of 0.18$^{+0.07 _{-0.06}$\,fm \cite{PhysRevLett.125.102501 , which is significantly lower than the PREX value, and for $^{48}$Ca a skin of 0.22$^{+0.02 _{-0.03}$\,fm, which in turn is significantly larger than the CREX result.}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Throughout this work we present a novel method to explore the variation of the neutron skin between two different isotopes of a given element with high precision and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-193: Furthermore, measuring both production probabilities for a given isotope simultaneously, many experimental uncertainties cancel or can be significantly reduced.

elements.section-2411-007: Sensitivity and Systematics of the Method\label{sec:sensitivity}
elements.sent-2411-222: The results for the xenon isotopes give an important hint on the sensitivity of double ratios to the thickness of the neutron skin.
elements.sent-2411-223: It is, however, highly desirable to investigate that relation systematically on more general grounds.
elements.sent-2411-224: Under theoretical aspects, one may use results obtained by a representative selection of nuclear models.
elements.sent-2411-225: That was the approach pursued in the previous sections: The RMF models incorporated into the GiBUU numerical transport package are well tested and widely and successfully used mean-field models.
elements.sent-2411-226: The results are typical at least for covariant non-linear energy density functionals.
elements.sent-2411-227: Since modern mean-field approaches lead within error bars to the same results for nuclear masses and form factors, the GiBUU-based result discussed in the previous sections can be considered of being valid and realistic even beyond the special class of built-in mean-field approaches.
elements.sent-2411-228: Obviously, extending the list to other covariant or non-covariant energy density functional approaches will not lead to fundamentally different conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-229: However, it is still of value to dig a bit deeper into the relation between antiproton-nucleus double ratios and the nuclear skin properties.
elements.sent-2411-230: It is worthwhile to recall that the rms-radius, together with the diffusivity, and the half-density radius, as defining key element of nuclear density distributions, are of high research interest.
elements.sent-2411-231: Most spectacular, this is reflected by unexpected results of the PREX/CREX experiments on the neutron skin in $^{208}$Pb.
elements.sent-2411-232: They are in tension with the seemingly well established former, largely theoretically based knowledge on that issue.
elements.sent-2411-233: In this paper and especially in this section we anticipate that antiproton annihilation on nuclei provides an independent approach on nuclear skin research.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Radial Scaling as a Form-Invariant Transformation\label{sec:statistics}
elements.sent-2411-247: The Fermi-function model is an ideal tool to clarify the connections between radial scaling and scaling of density parameters.
elements.sent-2411-248: Radial scaling by a factor $\alpha$ amounts to stretch ($\alpha > 1$) or squeeze ($0< \alpha < 1$) the radial coordinate: \begin{equation}\rho_q(r)\to \widetilde{\rho}_q(\alpha r)\end{equation} constraint by \begin{equation}A_q=\int d^3r \rho_q(r)=\int d^3r_\alpha \widetilde{\rho}_q(r_\alpha)\end{equation} which corresponds to require equality of the integrands, $d^3r \rho_q(r)=d^3r_\alpha \widetilde{\rho _q(r_\alpha)$.}
elements.sent-2411-249: The norm integral is conserved by using $\widetilde{\rho _q(\alpha r)=\rho_q(\alpha r)$ and $d^3r_\alpha = \alpha^3 d^3r$, or alternatively, $\widetilde{\rho _q(r_\alpha)=\alpha^3\rho_q(\alpha r)$ combined with the unchanged volume element $d^3r_\alpha = d^3r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: Modelling the nucleon density distributions by Fermi-functions, radial scaling produces \begin{equation}\rho_q(\alpha r)=) \frac{\rho_0(A_q)}{1+e^{(\alpha r-R_q)/a_q}}=\frac{\rho_0(A_q)}{1+e^{(r-R^{(\alpha)}_q)/a^{(\alpha)}_q}}\end{equation} with $R^{(\alpha) _q=R_q/\alpha$ and $a^{(\alpha) _q=a_q/\alpha$.}}
